passages.passage_text: The Government of India has announced extremely attractive incentives to promote the small scale industries (SSIs) in rural areas for the current year 1992-93. The support amount has been raised and fixed at Rs 30 lacs in comparison to Rs 25 lacs in the year 1991-92 and Rs 20 lacs in 1990-91. This provision is perceived by many as a very timely incentive in the right direction to motivate people for establishing many SSIs in rural areas.

The Union Government is planning to introduce a golden handshake scheme for its employees to reduce the size of bureaucracy. The plan is to be formulated in the context of the policy decision that no government employee will be retrenched while the staff strength of various departments will be surplus employees as a result of winding up of certain departments, which either redeployed immediately or transferred to the surplus cell units according to the new structure. Since the surplus cell be kept in the surplus cell up to a maximum period of six months. But the government shall, in the meantime, introduce various incentive schemes to encourage voluntary exit. In addition, the government is planning to reduce induction of new officers.

In the forties, nationalisation was considered the panacea for all socio-economic ills. Today, privatisation has become the buzzword that has been sweeping both the developed and the developing world for more than two decades now. Even in India, the idea has been gathering momentum. But, before the idea is transplanted in India, there are several aspects of privatisation that need to be understood. It would be worth while, in this context, to look at the experiences of other countries.

Even 'mild' and 'moderate' degrees of malnutrition could impair muscular efficiency and intellectual development of children. This was revealed in a study undertaken by the department of paediatrics of Banaras Hindu University for the Nutrition Foundation of India. The study covered 1,136 children in the age group of six and eight years in 22 schools in the Kashi Vidyapeeth block of Varanasi. They were investigated with a view to determining the relationship between their nutritional status and functional competence.

One of the important facts of the history of science is that major discoveries have been made by the youth. It proves that the most important requirement for success in scientific research is the freshness of outlook, associated with open mind and not the maturity of knowledge associated with age and experience. Conservation is a hindrance factor in scientific achievement, but it is useful so far as it puts brakes on wilder flights of youthful imagination.

Jindal city Tamil Nadu has a fairly extensive mid-day meal scheme for school children. A mid-day meal scheme is not a perfect solution to the malnutrition problem but has the added advantage of providing an incentive to parents to send their kids to school.

The serious accident in which a person was run down by a car yesterday has again focused attention on the most unsatisfactory state of street lighting. Now one expects the roads to be provided with the same standard of lighting as a main road but unless the council is prepared to make good its promises as regards road lighting, it will only be a question of time before there are further and perhaps more fatal, accidents.

solutions.solution: Directly follows from the passage. This is a relatively easy job. All we have to check in this case is whether a given inference is a direct consequence of something given in the passage. If it is so, the inference will be 'definitely true', just opposite to it could be the case of does not follow from the passage. In this case, the given inference could be just the opposite of something clearly mentioned in the passage. If it is so, the inference will be 'definitely false'. The following examples illustrate this point. Ex 1: Refer to sample passage 2. Inference: Many employees would be retrenched as a result of golden handshake scheme. Explanation: The passage clearly mentions that the government is reducing the size of its staff while seeing to it that no government employee would be retrenched. The given inference is simply opposite to it. Hence, it is definitely false. Ex 2: Refer to sample passage 3. Inference: India is also thinking of privatisation. Explanation: The sentence "Even in India ..., momentum." clearly and directly implies that India is also thinking of privatisation. Hence, the given inference is definitely true. Ex 3: Refer to sample passage 4. Inference: 1136 boys of 22 schools were taught by Banaras University. Explanation: It is an exact restatement of one of the sentences of the passage. Hence, it is definitely true.
